coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic remediation of fluoranthene contaminated soil by eco-friendly GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst: Efficiency, influence factors, mechanism, and toxicity analysis

coredata.dc:description: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), as a persistent organic pollutant, have adverse effects on soil environment and human health. In this study, a new type of iron-based material graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/TiO2/α-FeOOH was used to construct a photocatalytic system to achieve effective degradation of fluoranthene in soil. The hydrothermal method and sol–gel method were used to synthesize GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst, which exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity by promoting charge transfer, increasing specific surface area, and broadening light absorption range. The degradation ratio of fluoranthene in soil suspension by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was 67.37 % under the condition of 24 h of simulated sunlight irradiation, water/soil ratio of 10:1, and catalyst dosage of 50 mg/g. •O2 – and h+ played the major roles in degradation process, and the catalytic mechanism was proposed. The degradation products and degradation pathway of fluoranthene were analyzed by GC–MS detection and DFT calculation, and the toxicity of degradation products was predicted by simulation calculation. The effect of remedial soil on seed germination was investigated through germination test of tomato seeds, and results showed that the productivity of fluoranthene contaminated soil was recovered in a certain extent.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic remediation of fluoranthene contaminated soil by eco-friendly GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst: Efficiency, influence factors, mechanism, and toxicity analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), as a persistent organic pollutant, have adverse effects on soil environment and human health. In this study, a new type of iron-based material graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/TiO2/α-FeOOH was used to construct a photocatalytic system to achieve effective degradation of fluoranthene in soil. The hydrothermal method and sol–gel method were used to synthesize GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst, which exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity by promoting charge transfer, increasing specific surface area, and broadening light absorption range. The degradation ratio of fluoranthene in soil suspension by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was 67.37 % under the condition of 24 h of simulated sunlight irradiation, water/soil ratio of 10:1, and catalyst dosage of 50 mg/g. •O2 – and h+ played the major roles in degradation process, and the catalytic mechanism was proposed. The degradation products and degradation pathway of fluoranthene were analyzed by GC–MS detection and DFT calculation, and the toxicity of degradation products was predicted by simulation calculation. The effect of remedial soil on seed germination was investigated through germination test of tomato seeds, and results showed that the productivity of fluoranthene contaminated soil was recovered in a certain extent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few decades, photocatalysis technique has received considerable attention due to its potential in solving environment and energy issues [1]. Nevertheless, the preparation of photocatalysts with high electron transfer efficiency remains a great challenge. Interfacial design and co-catalyst effect are considered to be effective strategies to achieve efficient electron transfer in composite photocatalysts [2].

Therefore, the construction of composite photocatalysts with Z-scheme, S-scheme, or type-II heterostructures was used to improve charges separation and transfer [3]. Z- and S-scheme heterojunction can endow photocatalyst with strong oxidation–reduction ability and efficient charges separation simultaneously [4,5]. The advantages of Z- and S-scheme heterostructures are the higher oxidation–reduction potentials and faster separation of photo-induced carriers [6]. However, amounts of photogenerated carriers are recombined at the interface of composite catalyst due to its inherent charge transfer pathway, which reduces the utilization of photogenerated carriers. For type II heterojunction system, because of the cascade band structure photogenerated electrons and holes can be separated spatially, which can improve utilization efficiency of electrons and holes. But, the oxidation–reduction reaction ability of photoexcited carriers is reduced for type-II heterojunction [7,8].

As is known to all, TiO2 is one of the most popular and widely used photocatalyst due to its low cost, nontoxicity, and high photocatalytic performance under ultraviolet irradiation. However, fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and narrow range of light absorption limit its photocatalytic efficiency [9]. TiO2 is a classic catalyst with easily modified electronic structure to form heterojunction [10]. Iron (hydr) oxides photocatalysts, such as goethite (FeOOH) and hematite (Fe2O3), have attracted much attention due to its eco-friendliness, non-toxic, and high abundance. Among them, α-FeOOH has moderate energy gap (Eg) range of 2.0–2.6 eV, which allows visible-light-driven photocatalysis. Therefore, α-FeOOH has been investigated as an efficient heterogeneous catalyst for organic pollutant degradation and water treatment. Unfortunately, the catalytic degradation efficiency of pristine α-FeOOH is inhibited by low separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers due to inherently poor electrical conductivity. Previous studies demonstrated that α-FeOOH in heterojunctions could act as cocatalysts for various reactions. Thus, in order to enhance the photocatalytic activity of α-FeOOH, many studies focused on coupling α-FeOOH with other optimum materials to construct heterostructures to obtain efficient electron transter [11,12].

Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots, exhibit eminent charge transfer capability, which was widely used as electron transfer intermediary due to their excellent electronic conductivity and suitable work function [13]. Among them, GQDs has attracted enormous attention owing to its excellent electron accepting, storing and transferring ability [14]. Specifically, because of the unique mono-layered two-dimensional morphology and band gap structure, GQDs can be used to modify semiconductor photocatalysts to improve photocatalytic performance in hydrogen evolution or catalytic degradation of organic pollutants. In system, GQDs can serve as solid-state electron mediator to shuttle electrons between two semiconductors [15]. Therefore, GQDs is a promising candidate as charge transmission bridge to construct dual semiconductor photocatalytic system. Some composite photocatalysts such as Ag2CrO4/N-GQDs@g-C3N4, g-C3N4/ZnTcPc/GQDs, and Bi2WO6/GQDs/WO3 have been synthesized, and results showed that the GQDs can transfer rapidly photogenerated electrons and modulate the migration route of photogenerated electrons, furthermore, oppress the photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination [16].

Considering the matching band potentials of TiO2 and α-FeOOH, it can be speculated that design of GQDs-mediated TiO2/α-FeOOH heterojunction photocatalyst would enhance the interface electron transfer capability of TiO2/α-FeOOH, which would improve the photocatalytic performance. As far as we know, there has been no related report. Therefore, in this study a novel GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was prepared by hydrothermal and sol–gel methods. In this hybrid system, TiO2 and α-FeOOH are coupled to form Type Ⅱ heterojunction. TiO2 and α-FeOOH play the roles of producing photoinduced electron-hole pairs, while GQDs acts as electron acceptor to transfer electron. In comparison with pristine TiO2 and α-FeOOH, GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH exhibited improved photocatalytic activity in decomposing organic pollutants. The effect of toxicity change on seed germination was verified by seed germination experiment. The degradation process and mechanism of fluoranthene in soil were studied, and the degradation products and toxicity were analyzed. This study will provide a new way to remove PAHs contaminants from soil environment.

TiO2 gel was prepared by sol–gel method. 12 mL tetrabutyl titanate and 20 mL absolute ethanol were put into a beaker to dissolve evenly. Under strong stirring, it was slowly dropped into 20 mL aqueous acetic acid solution and aged for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, transparent and flat TiO2 gel was formed. After dry processing at 105 °C, cooling naturally, grinding treatment, and calcination in Muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h.

A calculated amount of FeSO4·7H2O was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water and then poured into a Teflon reactor for reaction at 160 ℃ for 3 h. After reaction, the prepared sample was washed repeatedly with ethanol and water. Finally, the obtained α-FeOOH powder was dried in a vacuum drying oven.

Glucose was used as the precursor to synthesize GQDs through a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 2 g glucose was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water, and stirred for 1 h. Then the solution was heated at 80 °C for 6 h. After dialysis and freeze-drying, GQDs powder was obtained.

12 mL tetrabutyl titanate was dissolved in 20 mL absolute ethanol, and then the solution was added into 20 mL aqueous acetic acid solution under strong stirring. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was put in air for 24 h to form a gel. Then, under stirring condition, deionized water was added to dilute the sol. A certain amount of GQDs (0.67 wt%) was dispersed in TiO2 sol, and stirred for 5 min. Then, a certain amount of α-FeOOH (Fe:Ti = 1:4, mol/mol) was added into the dispersion solution and stirred for 2 h at 60 ℃, then, dried for 12 h at 80 ℃ (as shown in Fig. 1 )

Rhodamine B (RhB) was used as an indicator contaminant for rapid evaluation of catalytic performance of catalysts. Typically, 20 mg of the prepared catalyst was dispersed into 100 mL RhB solution (5 mg/L) under stirring. After stir for 1.5 h under dark, a 35 W xenon lamp was used to provide simulate sunlight. The reaction time was 2 h. At predetermined time intervals, 5 mL solid–liquid mixture was taken, then centrifuged for 5 min at a speed of 4000 r/min. Finaly, the absorbance of RhB was detected.

The crystalline structure was determined by powder X-ray diffraction (Bruker D8 ADVANCE). The morphology was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Gemini SEM 300), and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) attached to the SEM instrument was used to analyze the elementary composition. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + ) was measured to further analyze the composition of samples. The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20. The Raman spectra were measured by Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution. Specific surface area porosity analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2460) was used to determine the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas. The optical absorption properties were obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600). Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4700) under a wavelength range of 360–580 nm and an excitation wavelength of 315 nm.

Electrochemical properties such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), linear voltammetry (LSV), Mott-Sochttky curves, Open-circuit potential, and photocurrent density were measured by an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 4000A) using a standard three-electrode system. The Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) glass coated with prepared catalysts was used as working electrode, platinum electrode was used as counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrode was used as reference electrode. The electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution (details are shown in Text S1).

The physical and chemical properties of original soil were shown in Text S2. Before using, the debris in soil was removed. The soil was dried in air, and ground until the particle size is less than 1 mm. Then, the soil was subjected to intermittent autoclaving at 121 ℃ for 0.5 h, and another sterilization was performed after 24 h intervals. The treated soil (100 g) was mixed with 100 mL solution of dichloromethane and acetone (1:1), and ultrasonic cleaned for 30 min, then the supernatant was discarded. Repeating this operation for three times, and finally place the cleaned soil in fume hood until the organic solvent volatilizes completely.

Firstly, fluoranthene was dissolved in methanol, then the methanol solution of fluoranthene was added into the soil sample. The mixture of fluoranthene and soil sample was fully stirred. After methanol volatilization, a contaminated soil sample with fluoranthene concentration of 40 mg/kg was obtained. The contaminated soil was aged for six months before experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of fluoranthene in soil suspension
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared photocatalyst (250 mg) was added into the soil suspension (5 g soil) with a ratio of water/soil = 10:1. A 35 W xenon lamp was used as simulated sunlight in this experiment, and the light source was 12 cm away from the soil suspension. The degradation reaction was carried out in a stirred state by a magnetic stirrer for 4 h.

After photocatalytic remediation, the soil suspension was transferred to a centrifuge tube, and the supernatant was discarded after centrifugation. The residual soil was dried. 10 mL of acetone/hexane solution (1:1) was added into the dried soil, then mixed using a vortex oscillator. After 30 min sonication the supernatant was centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 r/min. 5 mL supernatant was purified by a chromatography column (4 g silica gel + 2 g anhydrous sodium sulfate). The specific purification steps were shown in Text S3. After filtration with 0.22 μm filter membrane, the fluoranthene was detected by HPLC. Liquid chromatography was performed on a column (C18, 4.6 mm × 250 mm). Mobile phase was 95 % methanol + 5 % ultra-pure water. The detection wavelength was 324 nm. The recovery rate of fluoranthene was 90 ± 5 %, and the accuracy of detection method was 3 %.

The quenchers p-benzoquinone (BQ, 5 mM), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA, 5 mM) and potassium iodide (KI, 5 mM) were added into reaction system to quench superoxide radical, hydroxyl radical, and hole, respectively. Other experimental conditions were the same as 2.11.

The tomato seeds with same quality were used to germination experiment. They were disinfected by hydrogen peroxide and rinsed with distilled water, then soaked in water for 5 h. Uncontaminated soil, contaminated soil (40 mg/kg fluoranthene), and remediated soil were placed in petri dishes, respectively. Each petri dish was filled with 20 tomato seeds, and cultivated in a greenhouse away from light. The greenhouse temperature was maintained at 25 ℃. The incubation time was 7 days, and the experiment was repeated 3 times. Germination rate, root length, and plantule length were recorded.

All calculations were performed by CASTEP and DMol3 modules in Material Studio software. The CASTEP module was used to optimize the molecular geometry structure. The spatial group of the constructed cell was P1, and the specific parameters were a = b = c = 20A, α = β = γ = 90°. Other parameters were default values. GGA/PBE function and CASTEP module were used to calculate Mulliken population and explore the break of chemical bonds in molecules. The Fukui index, electrostatic potential (ESP), highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and work function were analyzed by the basic set of DMol3 module [17].

The morphology, microstructure, and composition of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the self-assembled α-FeOOH was nanorod structure with length of 300–500 nm and width of 50–200 nm, and gathered in three-dimensional clusters. The separately prepared TiO2 was blocky structure as showed in Fig. 2b. From Fig. 2c it can be found that some granular TiO2 nanoparticles were formed on the surface of α-FeOOH nanorods. Fig. 2d and e presented the TEM and HRTEM images to further explore the microstructure and composition of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. Fig. 2d revealed the coupling of TiO2 and α-FeOOH, that is, the TiO2 nanoparticles were successfully deposited onto α-FeOOH and the rod-like morphology was maintained. The HRTEM image showed that the lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.245 and 0.35 nm were observed, which corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of α-FeOOH and (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2, respectively [18]. Moreover, it can be seen that there was a clear interface between TiO2 and α-FeOOH. These characterization results preliminarily proved the successful preparation of TiO2/α-FeOOH heterojunction. According to Scherrer formula [19] D=
k λ
c o s θ F W H M
, the average particle size of TiO2 can be calculated to be 10.382 nm. The unique surface and structure properties are conducive to rapid charge transfer and improvement of photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH [20]. In addition, no GQDs were observed on the surface of TiO2/α-FeOOH by SEM and TEM characterizations due to its low loading and high distribution. The composition of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was further measured by EDS elemental mapping. As shown in Fig. 2(f-j), the elements Fe and O were uniformly observed, and which location was consistent with the α-FeOOH nanorod. Meanwhile, the observed distribution of Ti and O elements was also corresponding to the deposition location of TiO2. Fig. 2j revealed the existence of GQDs, which further confirms the successful assembly of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH.

In order to further verify the element composition and valence states of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH the XPS characterization was conducted. From the survey XPS spectrum of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH (Fig. 3 a) it can be found that the Fe, O, Ti, and C elements were clearly observed. Their high-resolution XPS spectra were showed in Fig. 3(b-e). As shown in Fig. 3b, two binding energy peaks at 458.4 and 464.2 eV can be attributed to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, which manifests the existence of Ti4+ in TiO2 [21]. A main peak at 529.8 eV and a shoulder at 531.1 eV were observed in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c). The O with binding energy of 529.8 eV is attributed to the lattice oxygen of TiO2, and the peak with binding energy of 531.1 eV is related to the hydroxyl oxygen from α-FeOOH [22]. The Fe 2p spectrum displays 6 peaks (Fig. 3d), of which the binding energies at 710.4 and 723.8 eV belong to Fe2+ ion, and the binding energies at 712.5 and 725.8 eV correspond to Fe3+ ion. Two satellite peaks located at 718.5 and 732.6 eV were also observed, which may be related to Fe3+ oxidation states in α-FeOOH [18]. Moreover, Fig. 3e shows the C 1 s XPS spectra of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. The C 1 s binding energy at about 284.7 eV is usually attributed to the C–C/C = C. The peak centered at 285.9 eV is assigned to C-O bond. The oxygen-containing carbon groups C = O (288.6 eV) was also confirmed by XPS measurement [23].

The structure and composition of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH were further ascertained by XRD, Raman, and FTIR measurements. Fig. 3f shows the XRD patterns, it was found that XRD diffraction patterns of α-FeOOH and TiO2 are consistent with the standard card No.29–0713 and No.21–1272, respectively. The characteristic diffraction peaks located at 21.2°, 33.4°, 34.6°, 36.6°, 41.2°, and 53.1° can be indexed to the (110), (130), (021), (111), (140), and (221) planes of α-FeOOH [24]. TiO2 is mainly consisted of anatase phase, and the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25.3°, 37.6°, 48.1°, 53.8°, and 55.1° are well indexed to the characteristic peaks of (101), (004), (200), (105), and (211) planes, respectively [25]. The Raman spectrum of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was showed in Fig. 3g. Of these, the characteristic bands at 151 and 197 cm−1 correspond to the intrinsic anatase form of TiO2. And, the vibration bands at 217, 280, and 410 cm−1 can be indexed to the Raman spectrum of α-FeOOH. For pure GQDs (illustration in Fig. 3g), two obvious Raman peaks at 1350 and 1570 cm−1 can be indexed to D-band and G-band, respectively, while the peaks relating to GQDs was not observed for GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH due to its low content [18]. FTIR spectra was used to further confirm the composition of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH (Fig. 3h). The peaks located at 461, 613, 672, 798, and 901 cm−1 are caused by the vibration modes of FeO6 coordination octahedron. Of which, two peaks located at 613 and 672 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of Fe-O bond, and the flexural vibration peaks measured at 798 and 901 cm−1 for GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH and α-FeOOH are caused by bending vibration of Fe-O–H bond [26]. For TiO2, the wide band between 800 and 400 cm−1 is associated with stretching vibration of Ti-O [27]. The absorption peaks recorded within the scope of 1700–1100 cm−1 for GQDs and GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH are aroused by the stretching vibration of C-OC, C-O, C-O–H, C = C, and C = O [18]. Meanwhile, the vibrations for C–H and C-OH/OH at 2937 and 3403 cm−1 can also be observed, respectively [28]. The characteristic peak centered at 3144 cm−1 belongs to hydrophilic hydroxyls function groups (–OH) in α-FeOOH [24,29]. Comparing with single α-FeOOH, TiO2, or GQDs, the absorption peaks of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH weaken, manifesting that TiO2 and GQDs were not simply covered on the surface of α-FeOOH, but likely to exist strong electrostatic interactions between TiO2, GQDs, and α-FeOOH. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm was used to explore the pore structure and specific surface area of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, results were showed in Fig. 3i. It can be found that GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH reveals a type II isotherm with type H2 hysteresis loop, indicating its mesoporous structure with pore size in the range of 2–7 nm. Moreover, Table S1 shows that the specific surface area and pore volume of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH is 194.94 m2/g and 0.25 cm3/g, respectively, which are higher than that of TiO2 (56.64 m2/g and 0.17 cm3/g) and α-FeOOH (14.10 m2/g and 0.03 cm3/g). These results demonstrate that GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH has more active sites and a greater chance of contacting with contaminants, ultimately shows better catalytic performance than TiO2 and α-FeOOH.

Optical properties of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH were explored by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) and PL spectrum. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, the prepared TiO2 has strong light absorption capability in UV region with a clear absorption edge at about 400 nm. α-FeOOH exhibits broad and strong light absorption in UV–visible region and even in near IR region. Therefore, after assembling of GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH, the GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst shows enhanced absorption covering the whole UV–visible and near IR light region, and the absorption edge is red-shifted compared with TiO2 due to its broaden Eg, which is beneficial to improve photocatalytic efficiency [25,30]. According to the DRS results, the Eg energies were determined through a plot of (αhν)1/2 versus the photo energy (hν) (Fig. 4b). Results showed that the Eg value of TiO2, α-FeOOH, and GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH is 3.20 eV, 2.16 eV, and 2.19 eV, respectively. The room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) technique was performed to investigate the recombination of photoexcited electron/hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 4c, TiO2 shows an evident PL emission because of the recombination of light-excited carriers. It is worth noted that the formation of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH heterojunction although does not change the emission position, but dramatically quenches the PL emission intensity, suggesting that the coupling of GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH greatly inhibits the recombination of electrons and holes [31,32]. The reasons can be attributed to that GQDs and band difference between TiO2 and α-FeOOH could both improve electron transfer efficiency. The detailed mechanism will be analyzed later.

It is well known that photogenerated carrier migration and separation are the key factors in determining photocatalytic activity. Therefore, EIS analysis was used to evaluate charge transfer capacity. The semi-arc diameter indicates the charge transfer resistance, and the smaller the semi-arc diameter, the lower the charge transfer resistance [33]. Fig. 4d shows that the Nyquist curve of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH has a smaller semi-arc diameter compared with TiO2 and α-FeOOH, which indicates that the charge transfer resistance is the lowest and the photogenerated carriers transfer rate is the fastest for GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. The above phenomenon can be attributed to the strong electron interaction between GQDs and TiO2/α-FeOOH [34]. Fig. 4e shows the instantaneous photocurrent density under simulated sunlight irradiation. It can be seen that the photocurrent density of these materials is extremely stable under four irradiation cycles. The instantaneous photocurrent density value of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH is about 4.0 μA/cm2, which is significantly higher than that of TiO2 and α-FeOOH. Furthermore, the measurement of Voc was conducted to study the electron accumulation of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. Fig. 4f shows the Voc response of TiO2, α-FeOOH, and GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH under simulated light on/off conditions. The Voc of all materials increased positively and hold steady when the lamp was turned on. Under the condition of light off, due to electron-hole pair recombination the accumulated electrons were gradually released, resulting in the weakening of Voc [35]. Results reveal that GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH photoanode exhibits an obviously enhanced Voc value under continuous on/off lamp irradiation, which confirms that GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH has higher photoelectric conversion ability.

To further explore the photoelectrochemistry performance of GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH, the photocurrent-potential (J-V) curve was obtained by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements. As shown in Fig. 4g, GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH photoanode exhibits an obviously enhanced photocurrent density. Such increase of photocurrent density is because of the improved light absorption and charge transfer capacity [36]. According to the LSV results, the photoelectric conversion efficiency ABPE of photoelectrode was calculated by the following formula:
ABPE
( % )
=
J ×
( 1.23 - V
RHE
)
P
light
× 100
Where J represents the photocurrent density (mA/cm2), Plight represents the optical power density of irradiation on the surface of photoelectrode (mW/cm2), and VRHE represents the applied bias (V)[37]. As shown in Fig. 4h, the maximum ABPE of GQDS/TiO2/α-FeOOH system is 1.71 % at 0.13 V (vs. RHE), while the maximum ABPE of TiO2 and α-FeOOH systems is only 1.42 % at 0.30 V (vs. RHE) and 1.02 % at 0.44 V (vs. RHE), respectively. The increase in ABPE indicates the increase in utilization and conversion rate of photogenerated carriers [38]. These results mean that GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH material has an increased efficiency in generation and transfer of photogenerated charges [39].

Fig. 4i shows the Mott-Schottky curves of TiO2 and α-FeOOH. Firstly, the information on band structure can be obtained from Mott-Schottky curves. The positive slope over the test range indicates that both TiO2 and α-FeOOH are n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potential (E fb) of TiO2 and α-FeOOH was determined to be −0.16 and −0.60 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) by extrapolating the x-intercepts of Mott-Schottky plot. In general, the conduction band potential (E CB) of n-type semiconductor is more negative than the E fb about 0.2 V (vs. NHE), and the conversion formula between E(NHE) and E(Ag/AgCl) is E(NHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.197. Therefore, it can be considered that the values of E fb (vs. Ag/AgCl) and E CB (vs. NHE) are approximately equal, that is, the E CB (vs. NHE) of TiO2 and α-FeOOH is −0.16 V and −0.60 V, respectively. Furtherly, the valence band maximum energy (E VB) of TiO2 and α-FeOOH was calculated to be 3.04 V and 1.56 V (vs. NHE) by the equation Eg = E VB − E CB, respectively [32]. Moreover, the slope of the linear region of Mott-Schottky plot is related to carrier density (Nd), and a smaller slope means a larger Nd [39]. So, the Nd of TiO2 and α-FeOOH was calculated (Eq. S1), which was to be 1.9 × 1021 cm−3 and 5.5 × 1020 cm−3 for TiO2 and α-FeOOH, respectively. It is clearly that TiO2 has higher Nd than that of α-FeOOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on photocatalytic activity and degradation of fluoranthene in soil suspension
Assessment of photocatalytic activity and stability of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under simulated sunlight, results were showed in Fig. 5 . From Fig. 5a it can be found that adsorption–desorption equilibrium was achieved after stir for 90 min in dark, and GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH showed the highest adsorption capacity. As shown, the pristine α-FeOOH, TiO2, and P25 exhibited a poor degradation activity after photocatalytic reaction for 120 min, however, the composite photocatalyst GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH showed a significant enhanced photocatalytic activity with the highest degradation efficiency as high as 98.18 %. The photocatalytic stability of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was also evaluated through recycling experiments on photocatalytic degradation of RhB under identical conditions. Results (Fig. 5b) showed that after five cycles the photocatalytic activity of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was not significantly reduced, which reached 87.66 % of the original performance of the first round. Above results indicate that the synthetic composite photocatalyst GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH has outstanding photocatalytic activity and stability. To explore the reasons for the improved photocatalytic activity of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, the microstructure, material composition, and photoelectrochemical properties of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH were tested, and the results were as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water/soil ratio on the degradation rate of fluoranthene in soil suspension
ce:section.ce:para: There are two main reasons that limit the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of organic pollutants in solid-phase soil. One is the limited light penetration. The penetration depth of light in solid-phase soil is less than 0.5 mm. Secondly, the low mass transfer efficiency in solid-phase soil is also a key factor affecting the photocatalytic remediation of soil. Therefore, to improve photocatalytic soil remediation effect, water and soil were mixed in different proportions to construct suspension system for photocatalytic degradation of fluoranthene. The catalyst dosage was 25 mg/g and the irradiation time was 4 h (35 W). As shown in Fig. 5c, the degradation efficiency increased gradually with the gradual increase of water/soil ratio from 0 to 10/1. When the water/soil ratio was 10:1, the promotion effect was the most obvious, and the degradation ratio of fluoranthene was 30.42 % after 4 h of light exposure, which was about 3 times higher than that in solid-phase soil. The reasons may be related to light penetration ability and mass transfer efficiency. The addition of water improves the light transmittance of reaction system and increases the light absorption of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. Meanwhile, the addition of water increases the mass transfer efficiency of fluoranthene, which is conducive to the contact reaction between fluoranthene molecules and catalyst [40]. The degradation ratio of fluoranthene decreased with the further increase of water/soil ratio to 15/1 and 20/1. The reason is that photocatalyst is suspended in soil suspension, when the water content is too much the exposure probability between photocatalyst and fluoranthene is decreased, leading to the reduction of degradation rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst dosage on the degradation rate of fluoranthene in soil suspension
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal catalyst dose was investigated by changing the catalyst concentration. The addition amount of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was set to be 10, 25, 50 and 100 mg/g (catalyst/soil, w/w), respectively, and the results were showed in Fig. 5d. It can be found that the degradation rate of fluoranthene increased with the increase of catalyst dosage from 10 to 50 mg/g, and the degradation ratio of fluoranthene was 38.43 % after 4 h of illumination when the catalyst dose was 50 mg/g. After that, the degradation rate was inhibited when the photocatalyst amount was increased to 100 mg/g. The increase in degradation rate may be due to the increased active sites on the surface of photocatalyst, which accelerates the degradation of fluoranthene by enhancing the production of active species. However, excessive photocatalyst increases the turbidity of reaction system, and reduces the yield of active species by inhibiting the light penetration ability, so excessive photocatalyst reduces the degradation rate of fluoranthene [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the degradation rate of fluoranthene in soil suspension
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of initial pH value of soil suspension on photocatalytic degradation of fluoranthene was studied, and the results were showed in Fig. 5e. Degradation experiments were carried out at pH 3, 6, and 10. At pH = 6, the degradation ratio of fluoranthene was about 35.0 %, which was higher than that of acidic and alkaline conditions. On the one hand, the change in pH will affect the surface charge properties of catalyst and soil, and then affect the mass transfer process of fluoranthene and the adsorption capacity of catalyst for fluoranthene. The photocatalytic activity is strictly depended on the capacity of pollutant molecules to be adsorbed on catalyst surface [42]. The TiO2 surface is positively charged under acidic media, which inhibits the adsorption of fluoranthene on the surface of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, resulting in a decrease in degradation efficiency [43]. On the other hand, the numbers of active sites on the surface of photocatalyst may be affected by pH. Under neutral pH condition, the adsorption capacity for fluoranthene and the numbers of active sites are appropriately enhanced, thus improving the degradation efficiency [41].

As shown in Fig. 5f, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TiO2, α-FeOOH, and GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH on fluoranthene in soil suspension was investigated under same degradation conditions. It was found that the degradation ratio was 8.35 %, 5.10 %, and 30.86 % for TiO2, α-FeOOH, and GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, respectively. GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH showed a better degradation effect, and the degradation ratio was 3.7 times and 6.0 times as high as that of TiO2 and α-FeOOH, respectively. Results further confirm that the coupling of GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH could significantly improve photocatalytic degradation activity. In addition, Table 1 showed the comparison of degradation performance on pollutants in soil by different technical systems. It can be found that GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH photocatalysis technology has advantages over most literature reports in terms of catalyst dosage, reaction time, and degradation efficiency.

The active species •O2 –, •OH, and h+ were believed to play a major role in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [47]. Therefore, the active species were determined by radicals capture test, which was helpful to understand the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of fluoranthene in soil suspension. As shown in Fig. 6 a, after irradiation of 4 h the degradation ratio of fluoranthene was 30.86 % without any quenching agent. However, when BQ or KI was added into reaction system, the degradation ratio of fluoranthene was significantly inhibited, which indicated that •O2 – and h+ are the main active species produced by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH. In contrast, the reaction activity was almost not affected after adding quencher TBA, manifesting that •OH may not be the main active species in this degradation process. The characteristic signal peaks of •O2 –, •OH, and h+ were further detected by EPR detection method. Fig. 6b showed the DMPO-•O2 – characteristic signal peaks of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, α-FeOOH, and TiO2. It can be found that the signal intensity of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was significantly higher than that of α-FeOOH and TiO2, respectively, indicating that GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH produced more •O2 –. This result further confirms that the coupling of GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH promotes the production, transfer, and utilization of photogenerated electrons. The characteristic signal of 1:2:2:1 of DMPO-•OH can be observed from Fig. 6c. It is clearly that no •OH signal was produced for α-FeOOH because of its more negative bandgap, while TiO2 produced the strongest •OH signal due to its more positive band structure. After contact of GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH, the transfer of h+ between TiO2 and α-FeOOH leads to decrease of •OH production. For the EPR signal of h+ (Fig. 6d), the lower the signal strength, the greater the yield. GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH showed the highest yield of h+ due to its enhanced separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. In short, the EPR results are consistent with those of active species capture experiment [34,36,48]. To explore the electron transfer direction between TiO2 and α-FeOOH, the work function was simulated using DFT calculation (Eq. S2). In general, when two materials are combined, electrons shift from a low work function to a high work function [49]. As shown in Fig. 6 (e and f), the calculated work function of TiO2 and α-FeOOH was 5.55 eV and 5.38 eV, respectively. As a result, the Fermi level of α-FeOOH is higher than that of TiO2, indicating that photogenerated electrons will transfer from α-FeOOH to TiO2.

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was proposed (Fig. 6g). Firstly, the excellent optical properties of α-FeOOH contribute to the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH [29,36]. From Fig. 6g it can be inferred that a large number of photogenerated electron-hole pairs are induced on the surface of GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH photocatalyst under UV–vis light. The electrons in valence band of α-FeOOH and TiO2 are excited to transition to conduction band, leaving holes in valence band (Eq. (1). According to the detection results of active groups and theoretical calculation, the electron-hole transfer pathway conforms to traditional type II heterojunction structure. The photogenerated electrons and holes result in the formation of an internal electric field in GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH system. The internal electric field changes the band bending at the interface, and provides the driving force for separation of electrons and holes [50]. The photo-induced electrons transfer from the conduction band of α-FeOOH to the conduction band of TiO2 because the energy level of the conduction band of TiO2 is lower than that of α-FeOOH. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes in the valence band of TiO2 transfer to the valence band of α-FeOOH due to its energy level is higher than that of α-FeOOH, which results in the accumulation of holes in valence band of α-FeOOH. Because the valence band position of TiO2 (+3.04 V vs. NHE) is higher than the redox potential of •OH/OH– (+1.99 V vs. NHE), the h+ in valence band of TiO2 can oxidize HO– to •OH (Eq. (2). However, the accumulated holes in valence band of α-FeOOH could not react with OH– to produce ·OH because the valence band potential of α-FeOOH (+1.56 V vs. NHE) is lower than the redox potential of •OH/OH– (+1.99 V vs. NHE), which just provides strong evidence for the significantly decrease in production of •OH after coupling GQDs, TiO2, and α-FeOOH [51]. But, from above analysis (Fig. 6a and d) it can be found that h+ in valence band of α-FeOOH could directly oxidize fluoranthene molecules and contribute to the main role in fluoranthene degradation [51]. Due to the strong interaction of GQDs, a C-O-Fe bond is formed in GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH, which redistributes electrons at the GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH interface. As a super electron acceptor, GQDs can capture electrons from the conduction band of α-FeOOH, which promotes efficient migration of electrons from α-FeOOH to GQDs, improving electron utilization [52]. Meanwhile, the accumulated electrons on GQDs can react with dissolved oxygen to generate •O2 – through single-electron reduction due to the energy levels of GQDs are more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2 – (E = -0.33 V, vs. NHE)[53]. Finally, the main reactive species •O2 –, •OH, and h+ all participate in the fluoranthene degradation (Eq. (4).
(1) GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH + hv → e- + h+
(2) h+ + HO– → •OH + H+
(3) O2 + e- → •O2 –
(4) •O2 –, •OH, h++ fluoranthene → Degradation products

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed degradation products and pathway of fluoranthene
ce:section.ce:para: Intermediates of fluoranthene degradation by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH photocatalytic system were detected by GC–MS (The method was showed in Text S4). As shown in Table S2, a total of 6 intermediates (red) were detected in this reaction system. Furtherly, the degradation products of fluoranthene were analyzed by DFT calculation, of which, Fukui index, bond layout number, bond length, HOMO and LUMO orbitals of fluoranthene were calculated. A larger Fukui index indicates that the atom is more vulnerable to be attacked, and f + governs nucleophilic attack and f - governs electrophilic attack. The number of layouts reflects the strength of chemical bond between atoms. Generally, the smaller the number of layouts, the more easily the bond breaks. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the C(1) and C(10) bonds have large Fukui indices, thus, which are vulnerable to free radical attack to form hydroxyl derivatives. The numbers of C4-C11 and C7-C12 key layouts are small, indicating that they are easy to be broken. Fig. 7 b and c showed the HOMO orbitals, LUMO orbitals, and electrostatic potential distribution of fluoranthene. Based on the frontier orbital theory, the higher HOMO energy means an increased ability to lose electrons, which are more vulnerable to be attacked by •OH, meanwhile, a lower LUMO energy means higher ability to obtain electrons, which are susceptible to nucleophilic attacks. Atoms on benzene ring C1, C4, C7, and C10 are susceptible to electrophilic attack, while atoms C6, C9, C13, and C16 on benzene ring are vulnerable to nucleophilic attack [54].

Therefore, combining GC–MS detection and DFT calculation, the degradation pathway of fluoranthene was proposed (Fig. 7d). The C9 and C10 on aromatic ring site of fluoranthene were the first to be attacked by free radicals, through electron transfer and hydroxylation the product 1 (m/z = 234) was produced. Product 1 was further transformed into product 2 (m/z = 234) by ketonization. Subsequently, product 3 (m/z = 236) was formed by open loop reaction and oxidation, and which was further oxidated through carboxylation to produce product 4 (m/z = 268). Then, product 4 was converted to product 5 (m/z = 182) by decarboxylation and hydroxyl substitution. Product 6 (m/z = 180) was formed by ketonization. This phenomenon indicated that fluoranthene was degraded to low molecular weight intermediates after radical attack [55]. Subsequently, Product 6 was transformed to Product 7 (m/z = 162) through deoxidation and ortho-cleavage reactions. Product 8 (m/z = 194) was produced through oxidation and ring-opening reactions induced by •OH. Hydroxylation and dehydration resulted in the formation of product 9 (m/z = 166) and product 10 (m/z = 148). In addition, product 8 can also be directly oxidized to short chain small molecule product 11 (m/z = 130) by ring opening, decarboxylation reaction, and substitution reaction. Finally, product 9 and 10 was hydroxylated, isomerized, decarboxylated, and oxidized to form product 14 (m/z = 108) and 15 (m/z = 108). These products will be further oxidized into short chain small organic molecules, H2O, and CO2 by ring opening and carboxylation.

The toxicity of fluoranthene and its intermediates were predicted by bioaccumulation factor and acute toxicity (Fathead minnow LC50-96 h) using quantitative structures-activity relationship (QSAR) of T.E.S.T. It can be seen from Fig. 8 a and b that the enrichment factors of intermediates decreased to different degrees compared with fluoranthene. The toxicity of intermediate product 3 was slightly higher than that of fluoranthene, and the toxicity level of other intermediate products was lower than that of fluoranthene. Therefore, the above-predicted results indicated that photocatalytic treatment by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH system can effectively degrade toxicity and alleviate the adverse effects of fluoranthene and its intermediates on environment.

To further evaluate the ecotoxicity of fluoranthene and its degradation products, the experiment of tomato seed germination was carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 c and Table S3, contaminated soil with fluoranthene significantly reduced the germination rate of tomato seeds. The germination ratio was only 38.33 % for contaminated soil with 40 mg/kg fluoranthene. The seed germination ratio for remedial soil was 70.00 %, which was significantly higher than that for non-remediated group (p < 0.05), while, this result had no significant difference compared with that for blank (uncontaminated) soil sample (p < 0.05). Germination rate is an important index to reflect seed vitality. The germination rate of tomato seeds for remedial soil was good, indicating that the remedial soil has the potential to grow crops again. The radicle length and plantule length of tomato seeds after growth for 7 days under different soil conditions were compared to evaluate the influence of soil remediation on seed germination. As shown in Fig. 8 d and e, the seed growth trend in blank soil was the best, and the length of radicle and plantule was 7.29 cm and 6.99 cm, respectively. Compared with blank soil, the seed growth trend in remedial soil was inhibited, and the length of radicle and plantule was 5.04 cm and 5.08 cm, respectively. However, the length of radicle and plantule of tomato seeds cultured in contaminated soil was inhibited by about 45.0 % and 35.6 %, respectively. As a key factor in seed germination, root plays an important role in plant growth, development, and reproduction. The difference of radicle length reflects the difference of seed germination quality. The germination rate and germination quality of seeds are closely related to the degree of seedling emergence and reflect the productivity of soil in some extent. According to above results, it can be seen that the germination rate and root length for remedial soil were not significantly different from that for blank soil (p < 0.05), indicating that remedial soil had recovered its original productivity to a certain extent [56–58].

A novel GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH composite photocatalyst was synthesized by hydrothermal method and sol–gel method. The addition of GQDs significantly improved the photocatalytic activity. Type Ⅱ heterojunction was formed between TiO2 and α-FeOOH. GQDs acted as electron acceptor, which improved the transfer and utilization of photogenerated elecrions. Under the optimal reaction conditions (catalyst dosage = 50 mg/g, water/soil = 10:1, pH = 6, reaction time = 4h), the degradation ratio of fluoranthene by GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH was 30.86 %, which was 3.7 and 6.0 times higher than that of TiO2 and α-FeOOH, respectively. GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH showed excellent photocatalytic performance, which was better than most literature reports. A total of six intermediates were verified by GC–MS detection and DFT calculation. •O2 – and h+ were the main active substances in the GQDs/TiO2/α-FeOOH photocatalytic system. The results of tomato seed germination experiment showed that soil remediation restored the original productivity to a certain extent and effectively reduced the adverse effects of fluoranthene on ecological environment.
